title: Pulse Palpation
content_markdown:
#### Indications
- As part of a normal patient assessment.

#### Procedure

- Locate the artery: ##### Radial Pulse
- Place two finders on the wrist under the thumb.

##### Brachial Pulse
- Expose antecubital fossa.
- Locate brachial artery in the medial aspect of the antecubital fossa.

##### Carotid Pulse
- Place two fingers beside the patient’s thyroid cartilage. 
- Press inwards and backwards gently against the neck muscle.

##### Femoral Pulse
- Place two fingers below the inguinal ligament, midway between symphysis pubis and anterior superior iliac spine.

- Count the amount of beats in 15 seconds, or 30 seconds if irregular, and record the amount of beats per minute.

- Record the rate, strength and regularity of the pulse.

#### Specific Notes
- In shocked states, a peripheral pulse (radial) may not be palpable because the blood pressure is low.
- Palpated pulses may differ from the heart rate seen on the monitor, this is to be recorded.
- Do not use the thumb to palpate a pulse.
- For paediatrics use either the brachial or femoral pulse points.

Radial pulse

Brachial pulse

Carotid pulse

Femoral pulse
